flags.detail: Email address is null; could not verify domain or naming convention.

Manny Maceda is the Chairman of a global consulting firm (Bain & Company). Outreach from a seed-stage VC regarding pre-seed investment focus is likely misaligned with the target's seniority and strategic role.

approved_email:
Manny, your insights at the 2026 World Economic Forum regarding the shift from AI pilot projects to scaling deployment for bottom-line returns resonated with our team at Kyber Knight Capital.

While the theoretical potential of AI is clear, the real-world friction lies in integrating these models into legacy commerce stacks. We are currently focusing our pre-seed investments on AI-native tools that bypass these specific labor-intensive bottlenecks.

Given your focus on the trajectory of labor-adjacent AI deployments, I would welcome your perspective on how you see these implementations evolving over the next year. Do you have 30 minutes available next week for a brief conversation?

Best,

Alex
Kyber Knight Capital
validation_notes: Title 'Chairman' of Bain & Company confirmed via Wikipedia and context. Claim regarding WEF 2026 AI deployment is supported by the CNBC-TV18 source. Outreach alignment is weak; Chairman of a global consultancy is not the typical contact for pre-seed VC deals.

Title: Manny Maceda URL Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda Published Time: 2017-11-20T18:05:50Z Markdown Content: From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia | Manny Maceda | | --- | | Born | 1963 (age 62–63) U.S. | | Education | [Illinois Institute of Technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Illinois_Institute_of_Technology "Illinois Institute of Technology") ([BS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bachelor_of_Science "Bachelor of Science")) [Massachusetts Institute of Technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology") ([MS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Master_of_Science "Master of Science")) | | Occupations | [Management Consultant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Management_Consultant "Management Consultant"), [Strategist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strategist "Strategist") | | Employer | [Bain & Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bain_%26_Company "Bain & Company") | | Title | Chairman | | Spouse | Lyra Rufino | | Children | 4 | **Emmanuel** "**Manny**" **Maceda** (born 1963) is a Filipino-American businessman. As a career [management consultant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Management_consultant "Management consultant"), he was named worldwide managing partner of [Bain & Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bain_%26_Company "Bain & Company") in November 2017[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-1) and Chairman in February 2025.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-Fortune-2) Maceda is [Bain & Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bain_%26_Company "Bain & Company")’s first worldwide managing partner of Asian descent.[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-Bain-3) ## Early life and education [[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Manny_Maceda&action=edit§ion=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")] Manny Maceda was born in the United States and raised in the [Philippines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"). He is the eldest son of the late Filipino politician [Ernesto Maceda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ernesto_Maceda "Ernesto Maceda"). He earned a bachelor's in chemical engineering from [Illinois Institute of Technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Illinois_Institute_of_Technology "Illinois Institute of Technology"), where he graduated with [magna cum laude](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magna_cum_laude "Magna cum laude") honors. He later obtained a masters degree in management from the [MIT Sloan School of Management](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MIT_Sloan_School_of_Management "MIT Sloan School of Management").[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-named-4) After his studies at the [Illinois Institute of Technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Illinois_Institute_of_Technology "Illinois Institute of Technology"), Maceda started his career in the engineering plastics group of [DuPont](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DuPont "DuPont") in 1984. Following this he attended a Master's in Management at [MIT](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MIT "MIT").[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-FT-5) During his master's at [MIT](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MIT "MIT") he was still "unaware of the existence of the management-consulting industry." While at Sloan, he persuaded one of his role models, [Jack Welch](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jack_Welch "Jack Welch"), the chairman and chief executive of General Electric Co., to speak at the school. During the speech, a man wearing a gas mask threw a grenade-like object at Welch's feet. He stopped speaking and Maceda, correctly assessing that the object wasn't a real grenade, picked it up and walked it out of the building.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-WSJ-6) Sometime later, Welch invited Maceda to lunch and introduced him to two top GE executives, the head of corporate strategy and the head of plastics marketing, [Jeffrey Immelt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jeffrey_Immelt "Jeffrey Immelt"), who would later become GE's chairman and CEO. Both offered Maceda jobs, but he decided to join Bain instead.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-WSJ-6) In 1988, Maceda joined Bain as a consultant. He specializes in "full potential" transformations involving strategy, performance improvement, customer loyalty and organizational effectiveness that result in meaningful long-term increase in enterprise value. He has worked with clients across a range of industries, including financial services, industrial products, consumer products, retail, telecommunications, energy and technology.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-GNW-7) Before being appointed as the new worldwide director, Maceda led Bain's global Full Potential Transformation practice and was a member of the firm's Management Committee, Global Operating Committee and Nominating Committee. Previously he served as chairman of Bain's Asia-Pacific region. Before this he was a global leader of the Performance Improvement and Reengineering practices and leader of the global recruiting team.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-named-4)[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-FT-5) Meanwhile, he also published articles, such as in the MIT World Series, and spoke at the [World Economic Forum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum").[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-8) In November 2017, Maceda was named the successor of [Bob Bechek](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bob_Bechek&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bob Bechek (page does not exist)") as the worldwide managing director of [Bain & Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bain_%26_Company "Bain & Company") when Bechek stepped down in March 2018.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-FT-5) Maceda stated that he wanted to focus on the development of Bain's digital practice. Maceda stepped down from the CEO role in summer 2024.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-Fortune-2) In February 2025, Maceda was named as the successor of [Orit Gadiesh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Orit_Gadiesh "Orit Gadiesh") as the Chairman of Bain & Company.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-Fortune-2) He is a member of the MIT Sloan North American Advisory Board. Maceda is married to Lyra of the [Rufino family](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rufino_family "Rufino family"). He and his wife and their four children currently live in San Francisco.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-FT-5) 1. **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_ref-1 "Jump up")**["Bain & Company elects Manny Maceda as Worldwide Managing Director"](https://www.bain.com/about/media-center/press-releases/2017/bain-elects-manny-maceda-as-worldwide-managing-director/). 2. ^ [Jump up to: _**a**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_ref-Fortune_2-0)[_**b**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_ref-Fortune_2-1)[_**c**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_ref-Fortune_2-2)Brady, Diane. Bain & Company Bain & Company's headquarters at 350 Boylston St, Boston , Massachusetts Company type Private Industry Management consulting Founded 1973 ; 53 years ago ( 1973 ) [ 1 ] Founder William W. Bain Jr. Patrick F. Graham [ 1 ] [ 2 ] Headquarters Boston , Massachusetts, U.S. Number of locations 64 [ 3 ] Key people Christophe de Vusser (Global Managing Partner) Orit Gadiesh (Chairman) Revenue $7 billion (2025) [ 4 ] Number of employees 22,000 (2025) [ 5 ] Website www .bain .com Bain & Company is an American management consulting company headquartered in Boston , Massachusetts. The firm is one of the largest management consultancy firms in the world and provides advice to public, private and non-profit organizations. [ 6 ] One of the Big Three management consultancies , Bain & Company was founded in 1973 by former Group Vice President of Boston Consulting Group Bill Bain and his colleagues, including Patrick F. Graham. [ 7 ] In the late 1970s and early 1980s, the firm grew rapidly. Bill Bain later spun off the alternative investment business into Bain Capital in 1984 and appointed Mitt Romney as its first chief executive. [ 8 ] Bain experienced several setbacks and financial troubles from 1987 to the early 1990s. Romney and Orit Gadiesh are credited with returning the firm to profitability and growth in their sequential roles as the firm's CEO and chairman respectively. [ 9 ] In the 2000s, Bain & Company continued to expand and create additional practice areas focused on working with non-profits, technology companies, and others. It developed a substantial practice around working with private equity firms. [ 9 ] Corporate history [ edit ] Establishment [ edit ] The idea for Bain & Company was conceived by co-founder William Worthington Bain Jr. during his time at the Boston Consulting Group (BCG). [ 10 ] [ 11 ] In 1970, BCG CEO Bruce Henderson decided to divide his firm into three competing mini-firms: blue, red, and green. Bill Bain and Patrick Graham headed the blue team. [ 12 ] The blue team accounted for over half of BCG's revenue and profits and won the internal competition. After the competition, Bill Bain grew increasingly frustrated by the wait for Henderson's retirement, the firm's project-based approach to consulting, and the refusal of management to help clients execute on the firm's advice. [ 10 ] [ 11 ] Around this time, he is quoted to have said to feel like "a consultant on a desert island, writing a report, putting it in a bottle, throwing it in the water, then going on to the next one." [ 12 ] Bain was the expected successor of Henderson within BCG in the early 70s. [ 10 ] [ 11 ] However, in 1973, three years after Henderson's competing team decision, Bill Bain resigned to start his own consulting firm. [ 12 ] [ a ] Most of the senior members of the "blue team" followed him to his newfound company, which was started from his apartment in the Beacon Hill neighborhood of Boston. [ 13 ] A significant part of the firms for which he was responsible at BCG also followed Bain to the new company. Within a few weeks, Bain & Company was working with seven former BCG clients; [ 10 ] this included two of BCG's largest clients, Black & Decker and Texas Instruments . [ 11 ] [ 12 ] [ 14 ] As a result, Henderson accused Bill of stealing BCG's clientele. [ 11 ] It is believed that the competition Henderson put out laid the foundation for Bain & Company. [ 12 ] Bain & Company grew quickly, [ 15 ] primarily through word-of-mouth among CEOs and board members. [ 16 ] The firm established its first formal office in Boston. [ 10 ] This was followed by a European office in London in 1979. [ 10 ] Bain & Company was incorporated in 1985. [ 12 ] The firm grew an average of 50 percent per year, reaching $150 million in revenues by 1986. [ 16 ] The number of staff at the firm tripled from 1980 to 1986, reaching 800 in 1987. [ 16 ] By 1987, Bain & Company was one of the four largest "strategy specialist" consulting firms. [ 15 ] Employee turnover was 8 percent annually compared to an industry average of 20 percent. [ 16 ] Some of the firm's largest clients in this period were National Steel and Chrysler , each of which reduced manufacturing costs with Bain's help. [ 10 ] Turmoil [ edit ] In the late 1980s, Bain & Company experienced a series of setbacks. [ 14 ] A public relations crisis emerged in 1987, due to a controversy involving Bain's work with Guinness . [ 10 ] Tension was growing over the firm's partnership structure, whereby only Bain knew how much the firm was making and decided how much profit-sharing each partner received. [ 16 ] The stock market crashed the same year, and many Bain clients reduced or eliminated their spending with the firm. [ 12 ] There were two rounds of layoffs, eliminating about 30 percent of the workforce. [ 12 ] [ 14 ] [ b ] The Guinness share-trading fraud began with Britain's Department of Trade and Industry investigating whether Bain's client Guinness illegally inflated its stock price. [ 11 ] Bain had helped Guinness trim 150 companies from its portfolio after a period of excessive diversification [ 16 ] and expand into hard liquor with the acquisition of two Scotch whisky companies, [ 10 ] growing profits six-fold. [ 16 ] During this time, Bain made an exception to company policy by allowing a consultant to serve as an interim board member and head of finance for Guinness. [ 11 ] [ 16 ] Bain & Company was not accused of any wrongdoing and no charges were pressed against the firm [ 17 ] for the manipulation of the stock price, but having a Bain consultant work as both vendor and client drew criticisms of its handling of a situation that involved a conflict of interest. [ 11 ] [ 16 ] In 1985 and 1986, Bain & Company took out loans to buy 30 percent of the firm from Bain and other partners for $200 million and used the shares to create an employee stock ownership plan (ESOP). [ 14 ] [ 18 ] These shares of the company were bought at five times Bain & Company's annual revenue, more than double the norm, and cost the firm $25 million in annual interest fees, exacerbating the firm's financial troubles. [ 14 ] Bain hired former U.S. Army general Pete Dawkins as the head of North America in hopes that new leadership could bring about a turnaround, but Dawkins' leadership led to even more turnover. [ 10 ] Bill Bain tried to sell the firm but could not find a buyer. [ 10 ] Rebound [ edit ] Mitt Romney was hired back as interim CEO of Bain & Company in January 1991 [ 18 ] and is credited with saving the company from bankruptcy during his one-year stint in the position. [ 19 ] [ 20 ] Romney had originally left Bain & Company in 1983 after being appointed to lead Bain Capital , [ 12 ] [ c ] an independent private equity firm that would buy companies that Bain & Company partners would improve and re-sell [ 12 ] and whose funds these partners invested in. [ 12 ] Romney allowed managers to know each other's salaries, [ 19 ] re-negotiated the firm's debt, [ 22 ] and restructured the organization so more partners had an ownership stake in the firm. [ 14 ] Romney convinced the founding partners to give up $100 million in equity. [ 18 ] Bain and most of the founding partners left the firm. [ 12 ] Orit Gadiesh Romney left again in December 1992 to pursue a career in politics, [ 10 ] but not before he organized an election the following year that lead to the appointment of Orit Gadiesh as chairman and Thomas J. Tierney as worldwide managing director in July 1993. [ 12 ] [ 23 ] Gadiesh improved morale and loosened the firm's policy against working with companies in the same industry to reduce its reliance on a small number of clients. [ 20 ] London-based Gadiesh has been serving as chairman since. By the end of 1993, Bain & Company was growing once again. [ 13 ] [ 20 ] The firm went from 1,000 employees at its peak, to 550 in 1991, and back up to 800. [ 20 ] The firm opened more offices, including one in New York in 2000. [ 10 ] From 1992 through 1999, the firm grew 25 percent per year and expanded from 12 to 26 offices. [ 24 ] By 1998, the firm had $220 million in annual revenues and 700 staff. [ 11 ] Recent history [ edit ] Bain created two technology consulting practice groups, bainlab and BainNet, in 1999 and 2000 respectively. [ 10 ] bainlab was originally founded as Bain New Venture Group. [ 25 ] It helped startups who otherwise might not afford Bain's fees and accepted partial payment in equity. [ 25 ] In February 2000, Gadiesh was elected for her third consecutive term as the firm's chairman, and Tom Tierney was replaced by John Donahoe as managing director. [ 13 ] Around 2000, the firm became more involved in consulting private equity firms on which companies to invest in and collaborating with technology consulting firms. [ 24 ] By 2005, Bain had the largest share of the market for private equity consulting. [ 26 ] By 2018, Bain's private equity group was over three times as large as that of the next largest consulting firm serving private equity firms and represented 25% of Bain's global business. [ 27 ] Bain & Company does not publish its revenues, but it is estimated to have experienced double-digit annual growth in the 2000s. [ 26 ] Although the market for management consulting was declining, the Big Three management consulting firms , including Bain & Company, continued to grow. [ 28 ] Bain expanded to new offices in other countries, [ 10 ] including India in 2006. [ 29 ] [ 30 ] Like the other big consulting firms, it began working more with governments. [ 28 ] Bain maintained a "generalist" approach to management consulting but created a separate specialist business unit for IT and technology. [ 26 ] In 2012, Robert Bechek was appointed CEO and was later ranked as the most-liked CEO in Glassdoor employee surveys. [ 31 ] On November 20, 2017, Bain announced that Bob Bechek would step down as the worldwide managing director. Manny Maceda , was elected to succeed Bechek as the worldwide managing director effective March 2018. [ 32 ] [ 33 ] In an interview with the Financial Times , Maceda announced a focus on the expansion of Bain's digital practice. "Bain & Company acquired FRWD, a digital marketing agency based in Minneapolis, in 2018. Additionally, it acquired an analytics firm, Pyxis, the following year. About us Bain & Company is a global consultancy that helps the world’s most ambitious change makers define the future. Across 65 cities in 40 countries, we work alongside our clients as one team with a shared ambition to achieve extraordinary results, outperform the competition, and redefine industries. We complement our tailored, integrated expertise with a vibrant ecosystem of digital innovators to deliver better, faster, and more enduring outcomes. Our 10-year commitment to invest more than $1 billion in pro bono services brings our talent, expertise, and insight to organizations tackling today’s urgent challenges in education, racial equity, social justice, economic development, and the environment. We earned a platinum rating from EcoVadis, the leading platform for environmental, social, and ethical performance ratings for global supply chains, putting us in the top 1% of all companies. Since our founding in 1973, we have measured our success by the success of our clients. The next phase of industrial automation is defined by scale. The constraint is no longer technology. It is execution. Hannover Messe 2026 brought that shift into focus. Across sessions and client discussions, one point held: value now depends on how fast solutions move from pilot to plant floor. Key takeaways: • The industrial stack is converging fast, with hardware, software, and automation forming integrated systems • AI is delivering on the shop floor today, not a repeat of the IoT cycle • The market is becoming multipolar, with innovation advancing across regions • The central challenge is deployment, scaling across factories and delivering measurable returns The conversation is moving forward. Business News WEF 2026 | Bain & Company's Manny Maceda on why the world is long on India WEF 2026 | Bain & Company's Manny Maceda on why the world is long on India Manny Maceda, Chair of Bain & Company, says AI is beginning to show bottom-line impact in specific functions like software engineering and customer care, with wider gains from productivity and revenue growth expected to materialise over the next one to three years as adoption scales. By Shereen Bhan January 22, 2026, 5:55:20 PM IST (Published) 9 Min Read Speaking to CNBC-TV18 on the sidelines of the World Economic Forum 2026 in Davos, Manny Maceda , Chair of Bain & Company , strikes an optimistic yet pragmatic note on India’s place in a rapidly fragmenting global order. Rejecting the idea of a rigid US-China binary, Maceda positions India as a major power in its own right—one that is benefiting from its ability to balance multiple bilateral relationships while strengthening its domestic market, infrastructure, and capabilities in areas such as AI and energy. He underscores that India has become one of the highest-priority markets for multinational corporations and global investors, even overtaking China in foreign direct investment. For Bain, this translates into a sustained commitment to India—not just as a fast-growing client market, but also as a global talent and capability hub. At the same time, Maceda flags the disruptive potential of AI for global capability centres, calling for foresight and resilience as companies navigate technological and geopolitical uncertainty. Read More | Bain & Company's Manny Maceda says CEOs must rethink playbooks for a fragmented world order Below are the edited excerpts of the interview. Q: Talking about India in the context of Carney's medium power thesis, do you believe the medium powers or countries who not firmly placed between this US China battle will, in fact, benefit from what is going on today? A: I would probably place India squarely. I would not consider you a small power, the world's largest population in the world, a very attractive market. So just the recognition of that, and I don't know that the world is as simple as it's two poles, and where are you? I guess that's part of his thesis. You have to play multiple sides. You have to understand and so the fact that India is building, let us call it multiple bilateral relationships with different countries, it's arguably, it's a good thing and it's the right thing, and you have to, you have to play that and balance it. By the way those are country strategies, companies are doing versions of that strategy too, because it goes to, well, where do I do business, which, which suppliers do I go to, where do I hire and move my talent? And for all that's happening, it's you and I have talked the last years India is a high priority market for the multinational corporates and private investors, relative to the other markets in the world. It is a great domestic market. It's upgrading its capabilities and infrastructure, in AI, in energy. China used to be higher foreign direct investment than India. Well, India passed China. And most people I know are still long on India, us included. Q: I know Bain is long on India, what does that mean as far as Bain’s plans for India? A: I think continuing to have it be a priority market to keep building and so if you think of any market that we are in as a professional global services firm number one is the domestic market. Indian companies are growing large, and they are operating not just in India, but they are going around the world. Number two, multinationals, they prioritise India. Number three, our corporate, our financial investor base, private equity firms, most of them will say, this is one of our top two or three markets in the world. And so if you say all of that, are places that we can help support and enable for India to grow, we will be there by showing up with more people, more markets. Also like many companies, we have a large capability centre here and that's why we have the highest headcount in India, after the United States. That's going to continue to be a good place, although that's also an interesting place. Going back to your first question, the impact of AI and tasks performed by Indian capability centres is probably something you want to think about and anticipate some disruption as well. But as far as we are concerned, we'll continue to invest in India, and I will keep coming to visit and we can keep having these chats. Q: Do you walk away from Davos as a concerned optimist or a cautious optimist? A: I am an optimist still. I legitimately do have concerns. This is a complicated time in the world, and when you combine the implications of global politics, the potential of AI, and even some of the things we haven't talked about, the intersection of those two, not just on things like combat, defence, weaponry capabilities, drones, security. hacking, etc. there's a lot you could be concerned about that we have also lived through and so building our resilient muscles. If we can scenario plan, multiple worst-case scenarios, and have an agenda that we do a few things to so that we are ready when they do happen, I think we will be better off. Q: You have been engaging with CEOs both in the lead-up to Davos and during the meetings here. What is the prevailing sentiment among them? Are business leaders saying they cannot afford to wait for global uncertainty—whether linked to President Trump or other factors—to ease, and must continue executing their plans? A: I would say that the thematic issues that CEOs have wrestled with actions haven't changed that much the last three or four years. AI is number one, the post global order and then we are in the middle of that shift is number two. And then on top of that, the more ongoing challenge of energy transition during scarcer higher cost of capital times. But the first and the second are the most intertwined. AI is now at the point where it's real are we deploying it at scale and the post global order, whatever that is or that means, intersects in ways that influences all of our not just our strategic choices, but our strategic scenario and contingency planning. Q: AI continues to be top of mind here and as you said, it's real. It is being deployed. We are moving now from pilot to deployment at scale. But the question still being asked is, what is it doing to the bottomline? It's not showing up on the bottom line yet. When will it start to show up in the bottom line? A: If you look at the valuation of companies, obviously the expectation is that all of these values will show up. I would say that probably 20% are already getting it into the bottomline. And it started with where does the bottomline show up? The most obvious places is it is not across the board, it's in very specific functions, tasks that and so when you have a lot of tasks that could be AI enabled in a way that the productivity is increased, that shows up some basic software engineering, customer care. In many cases, it hasn't scaled to more complicated tasks yet, because it's an organisational change, people have to adopt it and use it to capture it. On the flip side, whether it will show up, being underwritten with revenue growth and new ideas and new opportunities., both of these haven't happened yet at scale, but I would say the expectation, the optimism, the belief that all of these investments we are making will show up is very high and that it will be in the next one to three years. Q: Are CEOs still focused on AI from an optimisation point of view, from an efficiency point of view, or are we closer to using AI from really transformation purposes? A: I would say it's probably 50-50, and so if you look, well, what does transformation mean anyway for a company. I would say it is half and half that the easiest way to underwrite your use case. You are making a meaningful investment in additional technology spend in AI well, where will that come from? The most obvious case is, can I redeploy my past technology spend to it? The second is, can I get productivity out of it, back to these specific and does productivity mean labour efficiency that I can capture? That's a little bit where most of them started. The term transformation is hard, because native to AI companies that will start just like in past technologies might get there faster, quicker than an existing company that has to transform. But that is that probably the number one topic that's still discussed here. Now there is an overlap between AI and all the global issues. It shows up in the new technology stack, that is the AI stack of today because we know what the old stack was, what was the semiconductor? What was the PC, what was the software, what was the applications layer? The new stack starts with things that you didn't think of as part of the stack before, predictable, reliable energy, a lot of it. For data centres, big cloud service providers that had been mostly American companies a new layer called the large language model that's playing out. So, I do think the companies that are now deciding they will continue to invest in AI with the belief and expectation that we will capture the returns to that investment, either in cost efficiency or new markets. But it has to overlap with your geographic choices. _claims.claim_text: Manny Maceda emphasized at the 2026 World Economic Forum in Davos that CEOs are shifting their focus from AI pilot projects toward scaling deployment to capture real bottom-line returns.
